Demanding immediate compliance. The leader issues orders without explanation and often resorts to threats.

Seeking tight control. Rather than delegating authority, the leader monitors situations studiously and attempts to control every detail.

Focusing on negative feedback. The leader relies on criticism, rarely offers praise, and focuses on what people did wrong.

Despite its negative inclinations, the Commanding style can be used effectively to signal a major cultural shift. When one executive was brought in to change a money-losing division, he encountered a top management team that met in a formal, intimidating conference room with a marble table that “looked like the deck of the Starship Enterprise.” The setting stifled dialogue. To signal a shift toward openness, the new president had the room demolished—a clear command-style move with positive effects. His strong tactics worked because he attacked the old culture, not the people.
Effective execution of the Commanding style draws on three EI competencies: influence, achievement, and initiative. But to keep the style from going off track, a leader also needs emotional self-control to keep anger and impatience in check, as well as empathy to avoid running roughshod over people’s reactions. When a leader lacks these abilities, the style runs amok, and the dictatorial leader barks orders, oblivious to the emotional damage.







Impact on Climate
When to Use




Because so often misused, highly negative
In a crisis, to kick-start a turnaround, or with problem employees.



While these dissonant styles have their place, the key to great leadership is knowing that no single style is a silver bullet.
——————————————————————————–
3. Conclusion: Building Your Leadership Repertoire
The most important lesson from this guide is that the best leaders do not rely on a single style. They are versatile, capable of adapting their approach to fit the needs of the moment.
The six leadership styles are like “the array of clubs in a golf pro’s bag.” Over the course of a match, the pro senses the challenge of each shot, swiftly pulls out the right tool, and puts it to work. High-impact leaders operate the same way, seamlessly switching between styles depending on the people and the business situation.
Research shows that leaders who have mastered four or more styles—especially the resonant ones of Visionary, Coaching, Affiliative, and Democratic—foster the very best climate and achieve the strongest business performance. By expanding your own repertoire, you can learn to lead with resonance and get powerful results.

title: 6  Primal Leadership
section: 6.5 The Leadership Styles in a Nutshell
text:
6.5 The Leadership Styles in a Nutshell








Style
Impact on Climate
When to Use




Visionary
Most strongly positive
When changes require a new vision, or when a clear direction is needed.


Coaching
Highly positive
To help an employee improve performance by building long-term capabilities.


Affiliative
Positive
To heal rifts in a team, motivate during stressful times, or strengthen connections.

Positive
To build buy-in or consensus, or to get valuable input from employees.

To get high-quality results from a motivated and competent team.

In a crisis, to kick-start a turnaround, or with problem employees.

title: 6  Primal Leadership
text:
6.6 Executive Summary
This document synthesizes the core principles of Primal Leadership, a model that posits the fundamental task of a leader is emotional. Great leadership operates through the emotions, creating “resonance”—a reservoir of positivity that frees the best in people. Conversely, ineffective leadership creates “dissonance,” undermining performance by driving emotions negatively. The vehicle for this is Emotional Intelligence (EI), a set of learnable competencies that are more critical to leadership success than IQ or technical skills.
The model is grounded in neuroanatomy, specifically the “open-loop” nature of the brain’s limbic system, which makes humans susceptible to emotional contagion. Leaders, being the center of group attention, have a disproportionate impact on the collective emotional state, or climate. Research indicates that climate can account for 20-30% of business performance, and that 50-70% of climate is attributable to the leader’s actions.
Effective leaders master a repertoire of six leadership styles—four resonant (Visionary, Coaching, Affiliative, Democratic) and two dissonant (Pacesetting, Commanding)—and deploy them flexibly according to the situation. Developing these styles and their underlying EI competencies is a process of self-directed learning, which involves five key “discoveries”: identifying one’s ideal self, understanding one’s real self, creating a learning agenda, practicing new behaviors, and leveraging supportive relationships.
Finally, creating a resonant organization requires extending EI beyond the individual to teams and the organizational culture. This transformation begins with confronting the group’s emotional reality and ingrained norms, then attuning people to a shared ideal vision. Sustainable change requires a systemic process, driven from the top, that rewires not just individuals but the entire organizational fabric.

title: 6  Primal Leadership
section: 6.7 Part I: The Core Principles of Primal Leadership
text:
6.7 Part I: The Core Principles of Primal Leadership

6.7.1 The Primal Task of Leadership: Managing Emotions

Resonance: When a leader drives emotions positively, they create resonance. This is a state of synchrony where people feel “in synch” or on the same emotional wavelength. Resonance amplifies and prolongs positive emotional impact, minimizes static in interactions, and builds the emotional glue that commits people to a team and an organization.

Dissonance: When a leader drives emotions negatively, they spawn dissonance. Dissonant leaders are out of touch with the feelings of a group and create an emotionally discordant environment. Dissonance leads to preoccupation with collective distress, undermining the foundations that allow people to perform at their best.

The emotional impact of a leader has tangible business consequences. A positive climate—driven by a resonant leader—correlates with higher morale, motivation, commitment, and retention of talent. For every 1% improvement in the service climate, there is a 2% increase in revenue.


6.7.2 The Neurological Basis: The Open-Loop Limbic System
Leadership’s emotional impact is rooted in the design of the human brain. The limbic system, the brain’s emotional center, is an open-loop system, meaning it depends on external sources—namely, connections with other people—for its stability. This contrasts with closed-loop systems like the circulatory system, which are self-regulating.

6.7.2.1 The Primal Impact of Leader’s Moods
The concept of Primal Leadership posits that the fundamental task of leaders is emotional. Success depends not merely on what leaders set out to do—such as creating strategy or mobilizing teams—but fundamentally on how they do it. The impact of a leader’s moods and styles is central to this paradigm, as they are the primary determinants of the organizational climate and, consequently, business performance.

Emotional Contagion: The open-loop system means that individuals rely on connections with others for their own emotional stability, a process known as “interpersonal limbic regulation”. Because emotions spread irresistibly whenever people are near one another, a leader’s emotional state is contagious and influences the collective emotional “soup” of the group. Everyone watches the boss and takes their emotional cues from the top, causing a ripple effect throughout the company.

Performance Driver: A leader has the maximal power to sway everyone’s emotions. Leaders “manage meaning” for a group, offering an interpretation of a situation to guide the collective emotional reaction.

If leaders drive emotions toward enthusiasm, performance can soar. Upbeat moods boost cooperation, fairness, and overall business performance.

Conversely, if leaders drive emotions toward rancor and anxiety, people are thrown off stride. Negative emotions, especially chronic anger or anxiety, powerfully disrupt work by hijacking attention from the task at hand.

Emotional climate, which is heavily influenced by the leader, can account for 20 to 30 percent of business performance. Specifically, roughly 50 to 70 percent of how employees perceive their organization’s climate can be traced directly to the actions of the leader.


This emotional impact determines whether a leader creates resonance or dissonance.

Resonance (Positive Impact): This occurs when a leader is attuned to people’s feelings and moves them in a positive direction, generating a “reservoir of positivity”. Resonance refers to “synchronous vibration” where people are emotionally “in synch” with the leader. Emotionally intelligent (EI) leaders naturally achieve this, leading to mutual comfort, emotional bonds, and engaged followers.

Dissonance (Negative Impact): This occurs when a leader fails to empathize or accurately read emotions, undermining the emotional foundation of the workplace. Dissonant leadership results in emotionally discordant groups where people feel off-balance and perform poorly. Leaders who spread bad moods are simply “bad for business”. Toxic leadership, such as from abusive tyrants or manipulative sociopaths, can poison the emotional climate of a workplace.



6.7.2.2 The Impact of Leadership Styles
A leader’s chosen style—a set of coordinated activities drawing on underlying emotional intelligence capabilities—directly affects the organizational climate and measurable financial results. The best leaders typically utilize a flexible repertoire of four or more styles.

6.7.2.2.1 Resonance-Building Styles (Apply Freely)
The sources detail four styles that consistently foster resonance and boost performance:

Visionary: This style moves people toward shared dreams and has the most strongly positive impact on climate. Visionary leaders articulate where a group is going, allowing followers the freedom to innovate. It relies heavily on inspirational leadership, self-confidence, self-awareness, empathy, and transparency. It is especially effective when a clear direction or fresh vision is needed, such as during a turnaround.

Coaching: This style connects an individual’s aspirations with organizational goals, leading to a highly positive impact. It involves deep conversations focusing on long-term development, delegating challenging assignments, and building trust. The underlying EI competencies are developing others, emotional self-awareness, and empathy.

Affiliative: This style creates harmony and strengthens connections by valuing people and their feelings, often building loyalty. It has a positive impact on climate and is useful for healing rifts or boosting morale during stressful times. However, relying solely on this style can neglect necessary corrective feedback and tolerate mediocrity. Key competencies include collaboration and empathy.

Democratic: This approach values people’s input, building buy-in and commitment through participation. It leads to high morale and a positive impact on climate. It is useful when a leader is uncertain of the best path or needs viable ideas for implementation. It relies on teamwork and collaboration, conflict management, and influence.



6.7.2.2.2 Dissonant Styles (Apply with Caution)
Two styles are noted for their potential to generate dissonance if misused or overused:

Pacesetting: The pacesetting leader expects excellence and exemplifies it, focusing obsessively on doing things better and faster. Although it can yield brilliant results with highly competent and motivated teams, it often results in a highly negative climate when poorly executed. Excessive use leads to anxiety, burnout, and micromanagement, as the leader sets high standards but may be unclear about guidelines, leaving employees feeling distrusted and pushed too hard. It often suffers from a lack of empathy and emotional self-management.

Commanding (Coercive): This style demands immediate compliance (“Do it because I say so”) and typically has the least effective, most highly negative impact on climate in most situations. It contaminates mood, erodes spirit, and undermines commitment. It is only appropriate in genuine emergencies, during an urgent turnaround, or when dealing with problem employees. Effective execution requires high emotional self-control to prevent harmful outbursts of anger or contempt.

title: 6  Primal Leadership
section: 6.8 Part II: The Four Domains of Emotional Intelligence
text:
6.8 Part II: The Four Domains of Emotional Intelligence
Emotional Intelligence (EI) is the set of competencies that enables leaders to create resonance. Intellect and technical skills are threshold capabilities—they get a person into the leadership door. However, EI competencies are the distinguishing factors that separate outstanding leaders from average ones. EI is comprised of four interconnected domains built upon a foundation of self-awareness.

6.8.1 Self-Awareness as the Foundation of Emotional Intelligence
Self-awareness is defined as having a deep understanding of one’s emotions, as well as one’s strengths, limits, values, and motives. It is the cornerstone upon which the entire structure of emotional intelligence—and thus resonant leadership—is built.
The four domains of emotional intelligence interact dynamically, and self-awareness facilitates both empathy (a key social awareness competency) and self-management. If a leader’s emotions are out of control due to a lack of self-awareness, their ability to handle relationships will inevitably suffer.

6.8.1.1 Key Components of Self-Awareness (Personal Competence)

Emotional Self-Awareness: This involves reading one’s own emotions and recognizing their impact, and using one’s “gut sense” to guide decisions.

Leaders high in this competency are attuned to their inner signals, recognizing how their feelings affect their job performance.

They are attuned to their guiding values and can often intuitively grasp the best course of action, seeing the “big picture” in complex situations.

This awareness allows leaders to be candid and authentic, speaking openly about their emotions or with conviction about their guiding vision.

Emotionally self-aware leaders can spot rising anger as it crescendos, see the cause, and determine how to respond constructively, rather than letting anger build into an outburst. Leaders who lack this awareness may lose their temper without understanding why their emotions control them.

This competency also plays a crucial role in empathy: A person perpetually oblivious to their own feelings will also be tuned out to how others feel.


Accurate Self-Assessment: This means knowing one’s strengths and limits.

Leaders with high self-awareness are realistic—neither overly self-critical nor naively hopeful—and are honest with themselves and others about their capabilities.

They exhibit gracefulness in learning where they need to improve and welcome constructive criticism and feedback.

This accuracy allows a leader to know when to ask for help and where to focus energy in cultivating new leadership strengths.


Self-Confidence: This is defined as a sound sense of one’s self-worth and capabilities.

Knowing their abilities accurately allows self-confident leaders to play to their strengths and welcome difficult assignments.

They often possess a sense of presence, a self-assurance that lets them stand out in a group.




6.8.1.2 Self-Awareness, Values, and Decision Making

Clarity of Values: Self-aware leaders understand their values, goals, and dreams, knowing why they are headed where they are going. Since their decisions mesh with their values, they more often find their work energizing. Conversely, lacking self-awareness means a person is likely to make decisions that trigger inner turmoil by stepping on buried values.

Self-Reflection (The Telling Sign): A key sign of self-awareness is a propensity for self-reflection and thoughtfulness. Self-aware people routinely find time to reflect quietly, allowing them to think things over rather than reacting impulsively. Many outstanding leaders cultivate this thoughtful mode in their personal or spiritual lives, which allows them to act with the conviction and authenticity that resonance requires.

The Smart Guess (Intuition): Self-aware leaders access intuition—the essential ability to apply life wisdom in making business decisions. This is important because emotional memory banks help leaders judge information efficiently, showing that emotions are part of rationality, not opposed to it. Attuning to feelings helps leaders find meaning in data, leading to better decisions. The required inner attunement needed to sense this intuitive message is provided by self-awareness.



6.8.1.3 Self-Awareness in the Learning Process

The Emotional Reality Check: Discovering the real self involves taking inventory of one’s talents and passions—the person one actually is as a leader. This process requires self-awareness to overcome the “boiling frog syndrome” (the slow, invisible creep of complacency and habit).

Strengths and Gaps: The realization of one’s strengths and “gaps” (the difference between the ideal self and the real self) sparks the readiness to change. The ultimate goal of self-directed learning is to build on strengths and move closer to the ideal self, which requires self-awareness to honestly identify what to keep and what to change.

The Antidote to CEO Disease: Self-awareness, specifically knowing one’s strengths and gaps, is critical for leaders to break through the “information quarantine” (CEO disease) that surrounds them. Effective leaders actively seek out both negative and positive feedback to correct distortions in their self-perception.




6.8.2 Self-Management within Personal Competence

6.8.2.1 Key Competencies of Self-Management

Emotional Self-Control: This is the ability to keep disruptive emotions and impulses under control. A key hallmark of this competency is a leader who stays calm and clear-headed under high stress or during a crisis. Self-control is essential for leadership because negative emotional surges (like anxiety or anger) can overwhelm the brain’s ability to focus on the task at hand. Leaders who exhibit strong self-control are able to maintain a confident, enthusiastic tone.
This competency is crucial for mitigating the negative effects of the commanding and pacesetting leadership styles, as it allows a leader to channel potentially aggressive emotions. It helps a person override emotional impulses, freeing up mental energy for practicing new, more productive leadership habits.
Transparency: This involves displaying honesty and integrity and maintaining trustworthiness. Transparent leaders “live their values” and possess an authentic openness about their feelings, beliefs, and actions. Integrity, at a primal level, hinges on impulse control (self-control) and ensures that a leader’s actions are consistent with their values.
Adaptability: This is defined as flexibility in adapting to changing situations or overcoming obstacles. Adaptable leaders can juggle multiple demands without losing focus, are comfortable with organizational ambiguities, and are nimble in adjusting their thinking in the face of new data or realities. This is especially important in today’s ambiguous and rapidly changing business environment, where adaptability allows leaders to roll with changes and help the organization adjust.
Achievement: This is characterized by a drive to improve performance to meet inner standards of excellence. Leaders strong in achievement strive constantly for performance improvements, set measurable yet challenging goals, and are pragmatic in calculating risks to ensure goals are worthy but attainable. This drive is internal, motivated by the need to meet high personal standards rather than external rewards like money or titles.
Initiative: This is the readiness to act and seize opportunities. Leaders with strong initiative possess a sense of efficacy (that they can control their own destiny) and proactively seize or create opportunities instead of waiting for things to happen. This competency is required for pacesetting leaders, allowing them to exert an unhesitating, commanding tone when necessary, though it must be balanced by other EI skills.
Optimism: This involves seeing the upside in events. An optimistic leader can “roll with the punches,” viewing setbacks as opportunities rather than threats, and expecting positive change in the future. Optimism is neuroanatomically linked to the left prefrontal area of the brain, which inhibits distress-driving areas (like the amygdala) and fuels motivated feelings, enabling the leader to persist despite inevitable failures and frustrations.



6.8.2.2 Self-Management and Leadership Effectiveness

Public Consequences: A leader’s emotions have public consequences. Because emotions are contagious, leaders’ primary tasks are the “emotional equivalent of good hygiene: getting their own emotions in hand”. Leaders cannot effectively manage emotions in others without first handling their own.

Creating Resonance: Leaders who master their impulses and feelings, remaining optimistic and upbeat, radiate the positive feelings that build resonance and create an environment of trust, comfort, and fairness. This self-mastery provides the mental clarity and concentrated energy demanded by leadership.

Impact on Change: Self-management, especially emotional self-control and adaptability, allows leaders to effectively lead through times of turbulence and change. Leaders who have mastered their emotions are better able to roll with changes and help the organization adjust.

The “Cool” Factor: The core capacity of self-management is linked to the historical concept of being “cool”—the ability to manage one’s turbulent feelings while allowing the full expression of positive emotions. The most meaningful act of responsibility a leader can perform is controlling their own state of mind.




6.8.3 Social Awareness as Attunement and Empathy
Social awareness supports the leader’s primal task of driving resonance. Resonance, which is a state of “synchronous vibration” where people are emotionally “in synch”, is naturally achieved by emotionally intelligent leaders. The domain of social awareness makes this possible through emotional attunement.

Empathy (The Foundational Competency): Empathy is the ability to attune to a wide range of emotional signals, allowing leaders to sense the felt, but unspoken, emotions in a person or group. It involves sensing others’ emotions, understanding their perspective, and taking an active interest in their concerns.

Neurological Basis: Empathy stems from neurons in extended circuitry connected to the amygdala that read another person’s face and voice for emotion. This circuitry provides a steady stream of bulletins (“he’s getting a bit upset by that last remark”) that the prefrontal zone uses to fine-tune a leader’s response.

Limbic Resonance: This neural attunement is called “limbic resonance,” a “symphony of mutual exchange and internal adaptation” where two people harmonize their emotional state.

Impact on Leadership: Lacking empathy means a leader will unwittingly be “off-key,” speaking and acting in ways that trigger negative reactions. A person perpetually oblivious to their own feelings (lacking self-awareness) will also be tuned out to how others feel, confirming that self-awareness facilitates empathy.


Attuned Leadership (Resonance-Building): Empathy allows leaders to tune into the emotional channels that create resonance and to fine-tune their message to keep it in sync.

Contextual Appropriateness: By being attuned to how others feel in the moment, a leader can say and do what is appropriate—whether that means calming fears, assuaging anger, or joining in good spirits.

Sensing Shared Values: This attunement also enables a leader to sense the shared values and priorities that can guide the group.

Empathetic Leaders are Effective: Leaders who grasp others’ feelings access a potent emotional guidance system that keeps what they say and do on track, making empathy the sine qua non (essential condition) of all social effectiveness in working life.



6.8.3.1 Competencies Associated with Social Awareness

Organizational Awareness: This involves the ability to be politically astute, detecting crucial social networks and reading key power relationships. Such a leader understands the political forces at work in an organization, as well as the guiding values and unspoken rules that operate among people.

Service: This competence involves recognizing and meeting follower, client, or customer needs. Leaders high in service foster an emotional climate so that people directly in touch with the customer or client keep the relationship on the right track, monitoring satisfaction to ensure needs are met.



6.8.3.2 The Business Case for Empathy

Talent Retention: Empathy is key to retaining talent because it allows leaders to develop and keep good people. Dissonant leaders—those who lack empathy—are one of the main reasons talented people leave and take the company’s knowledge with them.

Customer Relations: Empathetic people are superb at recognizing and meeting the needs of clients, customers, or subordinates.

Global Economy: In the growing global economy, empathy is a critical skill for getting along with diverse workmates and doing business with people from other cultures, acting as an antidote to cross-cultural misunderstandings.

Decision Making: Empathy means taking employees’ feelings into thoughtful consideration and then making intelligent decisions that work those feelings into the response.



6.8.3.3 Social Awareness and Leadership Styles

Democratic Style: Empathy plays a role in democratic leadership, especially when the group is diverse, as it prevents miscues.

Affiliative Style: This style, which focuses on emotional needs over tasks, relies on empathy to keep people happy by caring for the whole person.

Coaching Style: Coaching requires emotional self-awareness and empathy, ensuring that leaders listen first before giving feedback and that the advice is genuinely in the employee’s best interest.

Visionary Style: The ability to sense how others feel (empathy) is crucial for a visionary leader to articulate a truly inspirational message; a leader who misreads people simply cannot inspire them.



6.8.3.4 Role in Team Emotional Intelligence
In teams, the collective equivalent of empathy—Social Awareness—is the foundation that enables a team to build and maintain effective relationships with the rest of the organization. An emotionally intelligent (empathic) team identifies other key groups (internal or external) and proactively fosters a good working relationship with them, which is a powerful driver of organizational effectiveness.



6.8.4 Relationship Management as the Synthesis of EI

Foundation of Self: Leaders must first have Self-Awareness of their own vision, values, and direction.

Self-Control: Leaders must use Self-Management to maintain a positive emotional register and control impulses, ensuring authenticity and integrity in their interactions.

Attunement: Social Awareness (Empathy) is essential to tune into the emotional channels of others, making the relationship management skills appropriate and “in synch” with the group’s feelings.

If a leader acts disingenuously or manipulatively, followers’ “emotional radar” will detect a note of falseness, dissolving the relationship into cynicism and distrust. The art of handling relationships, therefore, begins with authenticity, acting from one’s genuine feelings.
Relationship management is described not merely as friendliness, but as “friendliness with a purpose”: moving people in the right direction, whether toward agreement on strategy or enthusiasm for a new project.

6.8.4.1 Key Competencies in Relationship Management

6.8.4.1.1 Inspirational Leadership (Inspiration)
This involves guiding and motivating followers with a compelling vision or shared mission.

Inspirational leaders get people excited about a common mission and offer a sense of purpose beyond day-to-day tasks.

They embody what they ask of others and articulate a shared mission that rings true and inspires others to follow.

This competence strongly undergirds the effective Visionary leadership style, lending grand meaning to mundane tasks and maximizing buy-in for organizational goals.



6.8.4.1.2 Influence
This involves wielding a range of tactics for persuasion.

A leader’s power of influence ranges from finding the right appeal for a specific listener to building buy-in and a network of support for an initiative.

Leaders adept in influence are persuasive and engaging when addressing a group.

For example, one director, lacking formal team power, succeeded by convincing people through “appealing to their agenda”.



6.8.4.1.3 Developing Others (Coaching)
This competency involves bolstering others’ abilities through feedback and guidance.

Leaders adept at this show a genuine interest in those they help, understanding their goals, strengths, and weaknesses.

They are natural mentors or coaches who provide timely and constructive feedback.

This underpins the Coaching leadership style, which focuses on developing long-term capabilities and linking personal aspirations to organizational goals.



6.8.4.1.4 Change Catalyst
This involves initiating, managing, and leading in a new direction.

Change catalysts recognize the need for change, challenge the status quo, and champion the new order.

They are strong advocates for change, even in the face of opposition, and find practical ways to overcome barriers.

This is critical for Visionary leaders when they need to “smooth the transition” when changing direction.



6.8.4.1.5 Conflict Management
This relates to resolving disagreements.

Leaders who manage conflicts well draw out all parties, understand differing perspectives, and then find a common ideal that everyone can endorse.

They surface the conflict, acknowledge the feelings and views of all sides, and redirect the energy toward a shared ideal.

This skill is essential for the Democratic leadership style, where building consensus and quelling discord are key.



6.8.4.1.6 Teamwork and Collaboration
This competence focuses on cooperation and team building.

Collaborative leaders generate an atmosphere of friendly collegiality and model respect, helpfulness, and cooperation.

They draw others into active, enthusiastic commitment to collective effort and build team spirit and identity.

They intentionally forge and cement close relationships beyond mere work obligations.




6.8.4.2 Relationship Management and Leadership Styles
The effectiveness of the four resonant leadership styles (Visionary, Coaching, Affiliative, and Democratic) depends heavily on the visible skills of Relationship Management. For example:

Visionary leadership is fueled by Inspiration and the Change Catalyst competency.

Coaching relies on the Developing Others competency.

Affiliative leadership is characterized by Teamwork and Collaboration.

Democratic leadership uses collaboration, Conflict Management, and Influence to build consensus and buy-in.

title: 6  Primal Leadership
section: 6.9 Part III: The Leadership Repertoire: Six Styles and Their Impact
text:
6.9 Part III: The Leadership Repertoire: Six Styles and Their Impact
The most effective leaders master a repertoire of distinct leadership styles and switch between them as the situation demands. Four styles primarily build resonance, while two can easily create dissonance if used poorly or excessively.









Leadership Style
How It Builds Resonance / Dissonance
Impact on Climate
When Appropriate




Visionary
Moves people toward shared dreams.
Most strongly positive
When changes require a new vision or clear direction.


Coaching
Connects a person’s wants with the organization’s goals.
Highly positive
To help an employee improve performance and build long-term capabilities.


Affiliative
Creates harmony by connecting people to each other.
Positive
To heal rifts in a team, motivate during stress, or strengthen connections.


Democratic
Values people’s input and gets commitment through participation.
Positive
To build buy-in or consensus, or to get valuable input from employees.


Pacesetting
Meets challenging and exciting goals. (Often poorly executed, creating dissonance.)
Often highly negative
To get high-quality results from a motivated and competent team.


Commanding
Soothes fears by giving clear direction in an emergency. (So often misused, it creates dissonance.)
Highly negative
In a crisis, to kick-start a turnaround, or with problem employees.



The Resonant Styles

The Visionary Leader: Articulates where a group is going but not how it will get there, freeing people to innovate. This style frames tasks within a larger, shared mission, giving work meaning and building commitment. It is underpinned by the EI competencies of inspirational leadership, self-confidence, and empathy.
The Coaching Leader: Focuses on personal development by exploring an employee’s life goals and career hopes, linking them to their daily work. This style builds rapport, trust, and motivation. It is the least-used style but delivers powerful results. It requires empathy, emotional self-awareness, and developing others.
The Affiliative Leader: Prioritizes people and their feelings, striving to create harmony and build emotional bonds. This style is excellent for increasing morale, improving communication, and repairing broken trust. It relies heavily on empathy, collaboration, and conflict management competencies.
The Democratic Leader: Spends time getting people’s ideas and building buy-in through participation. By listening to employee concerns, this style builds trust, respect, and commitment. It is most effective when a leader is uncertain of the best direction. It depends on collaboration, conflict management, and influence.

The Dissonant Styles (Apply with Caution)

The Pacesetting Leader: Expects and exemplifies excellence, is obsessive about doing things better and faster, and demands the same from everyone. While it can work with highly motivated and competent teams, it often poisons the climate by leaving employees feeling pushed and untrusted. It can create dissonance if the leader lacks empathy and self-awareness.
The Commanding Leader: Demands immediate compliance with orders and relies on threats, not explanations. This “Do it because I say so” style is the least effective in most situations, eroding morale and commitment. It should be used only with extreme caution in genuine emergencies or turnarounds, and when all else has failed with a problem employee.

title: 6  Primal Leadership
text:
6.10 Part IV: Developing Resonant Leaders
Emotional intelligence competencies are not innate talents but learned abilities. Leaders can change and expand their repertoire, but it requires a specific, self-directed process.

6.10.1 The Challenge: CEO Disease and the Failure of Traditional Training
Leaders often operate in an information vacuum, a phenomenon known as “CEO Disease,” where people withhold unpleasant but vital feedback. The higher a leader climbs, the less accurate their self-assessment is likely to be.
This is compounded by the fact that most leadership development programs fail to produce sustainable change. They often target the wrong part of the brain (the neocortex, for analytical skills) instead of the limbic system, where emotional habits reside. This leads to a “honeymoon effect,” where initial enthusiasm fades and old habits return.


6.10.2 The Five Discoveries for Sustainable, Self-Directed Learning
Sustainable change in EI competencies requires a process of self-directed learning that engages the emotional brain. This process is structured around five key discoveries.

The First Discovery: My Ideal Self - Who do I want to be? Change begins by connecting with a powerful vision of one’s ideal self—a person’s dreams, values, and passions. This vision provides the motivation and energy required to undertake the hard work of changing ingrained habits. This contrasts with the “ought self,” which is based on others’ expectations.
The Second Discovery: My Real Self - Who am I? This involves gaining an accurate picture of one’s current self, including strengths and gaps. Because of self-delusion and the CEO disease, this requires actively seeking out candid feedback from multiple sources (e.g., a 360-degree assessment) to get a full picture. The goal is to identify the balance of what to preserve (strengths) and what to change (gaps).
The Third Discovery: My Learning Agenda - How do I get there? This is the creation of a practical, manageable plan for building on strengths and reducing gaps. An effective learning agenda is tied to a person’s own ideal, fits their learning style, and includes concrete action steps that can be integrated into daily life.
The Fourth Discovery: Experimenting with and Practicing New Behaviors. To change habits, the brain must be rewired. This requires bringing bad habits into awareness and consciously rehearsing new, better behaviors at every opportunity until they become automatic. This implicit learning happens through extended practice in real-world situations and through mental rehearsal.
The Fifth Discovery: Developing Supportive Relationships. Lasting change cannot happen alone. It requires a network of trusting relationships—mentors, coaches, peers, or friends—who provide a safe context to experiment, offer feedback, and affirm progress.

title: 6  Primal Leadership
section: 6.11 Part V: Building Emotionally Intelligent Organizations
text:
6.11 Part V: Building Emotionally Intelligent Organizations
The ultimate goal is to create a critical mass of resonant leaders, transforming the entire organization’s culture and climate.

6.11.1 From the Individual to the Collective
While leadership development for individuals starts with the ideal self, for groups and organizations, the process is reversed. Change begins when the group confronts its emotional reality and cultural norms. Groups are motivated to change only after they grasp, on a visceral level, the dissonance in how they function.

The Emotional Reality of Teams: Teams, like individuals, have an emotional intelligence, defined by their collective self-awareness, self-management, and empathy. The team’s EI is shaped by its norms—the unwritten ground rules that govern interaction. Emotionally intelligent teams have norms that support trust, cooperation, and effective conflict resolution. The leader’s role is to make these norms explicit and model them.

Organizational Transformation: A dissonant or “toxic” organization is often a product of leaders who fail to listen or who create a culture of fear. Transformation requires leaders to actively question the emotional reality and cultural norms. A process called Dynamic Inquiry—focused conversations with open-ended questions about feelings—is a potent tool for uncovering what people truly care about, what’s working, and what’s not.



6.11.2 Attunement, Not Alignment
Creating a resonant organization is not about mechanically “aligning” people with a strategy. It’s about attunement—a process that engages people’s hearts and minds, connecting them emotionally to a shared vision.
Emotionally intelligent leaders achieve this by:

Discovering the Emotional Reality: Respecting the group’s values, slowing down to listen, and engaging people at all levels in discovering the truth.

Visualizing the Ideal: Tuning into their own feelings to craft a vision, attuning others to it emotionally, and putting people first.

Sustaining the Change: Turning vision into action through modeling, creating systems (HR, rewards) that reinforce EI practices, and managing the organization’s myths and stories.

Sustainable leadership development must be a systemic process, not a one-off program. It must be a strategic priority driven from the top, engage the culture head-on, and build a language of leadership that permeates the entire enterprise.

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.1 Executive Summary
The modern view of project management (PM) emphasizes the creation of sustainable business value rather than merely meeting the traditional triple constraints of time, cost, and scope/performance. Project management is defined as the planning, organizing, directing, and controlling of company resources for a relatively short-term objective.
Critical takeaways and shifts in modern PM practice include:

title: 7  Project Management
text:
Shift to Value and Benefits: Project success is fundamentally measured by the desired business benefits and the value provided to customers and stakeholders, not just delivery within constraints.

The Systems Approach: Effective PM requires viewing the project as a collection of interrelated activities (a system) and integrating resources across functional lines (the vertical hierarchy) into a specific project structure (the horizontal hierarchy).

Organizational Maturity: Achieving continuous streams of successfully managed projects (excellence) requires a standard methodology and process foundation (maturity). This often involves developing a Project Management Methodology (PMM) and potentially a Project Management Office (PMO).

Flexible Methodologies: Due to varying project requirements and market volatility, rigid “waterfall” methodologies are often supplemented or replaced by flexible frameworks or Agile approaches that prioritize customer involvement and rapid adaptation.

Project Manager as Business Leader: Project managers must evolve from technical schedulers/planners to become general managers who understand the total operation of the company, make both technical and business decisions, and align the project to strategic goals.

Planning is Paramount: Planning, organizing via a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS), and establishing robust Project Baselines (scope, schedule, cost) are foundational to control and successful execution.

Metrics and KPIs: Measurement has expanded beyond traditional time and cost reporting to include Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) and metrics that reflect business value, often displayed in dashboards or scorecards.

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.2 Overview

7.2.1 Introduction and Understanding Project Management
Project management, rooted in the U.S. Department of Defense and heavy construction during the 1960s, initially focused heavily on budget and schedule compliance for unique or repetitive projects.
A project is defined as any series of tasks and activities that:

Have a specific objective, focusing on creating business value, completed within specifications.

Have defined start and end dates.

Have funding limits (if applicable).

Consume human and nonhuman resources (money, people, equipment).

Are multifunctional (cut across several functional lines).

Project Management (PM) is the application of knowledge, skills, and tools necessary to achieve project requirements. It uses the systems approach by assigning functional personnel (vertical hierarchy) to a specific project (horizontal hierarchy).


7.2.2 Defining Project Success and Constraints
Historically, project success was defined by the Triple Constraints (time, cost, and performance/specification). The modern definition of success is much broader and includes:

Completion within allocated time, cost, and proper performance level.

Completion within other competing constraints.

Acceptance by the customer/user, delivery of desired business benefits and value, and minimum agreed-upon scope changes.

Absence of disturbance to the main organizational workflow or alteration of the corporate culture.

The ultimate metric for project success is the creation of sustainable business value.


7.2.3 Trade-Offs and Competing Constraints
Trade-offs occur when one aspect of a project must be sacrificed or compromised to achieve another. While the traditional definition of constraints relies on Time, Cost, and Performance (Scope/Quality), Complex Projects face numerous competing constraints, including Value, Quality, Image/Reputation, and Scope.

Constraints vary in importance across the project life cycle. For example, scope might be critical during initiation, while time and cost become more critical during execution.



7.2.4 The Project Manager–Line Manager Interface
Effective PM often involves a dual responsibility structure where the project manager (PM) is accountable for what is accomplished, and the line manager (LM) is accountable for how tasks are accomplished.








Topic
Project Manager
Line Manager




Rewards
Give recommendation: Informal
Provide rewards: Formal


Direction
Milestone (summary)
Detailed


Evaluation
Summary
Detailed


Measurement
Summary
Detailed


Control
Summary
Detailed



Depending on the organizational structure (discussed in Chapter 3), line managers typically make performance evaluations, potentially utilizing input from the PM.


7.2.5 Project-Driven Versus Non–Project-Driven Organizations

Project-Driven Organization: All work is characterized through projects, each operating as a separate cost center with its own profit-and-loss statement (e.g., aerospace, construction). Everything centers on the projects.

Non–Project-Driven/Operational-Driven Organization: Projects are secondary to functional operations. Such companies often rely heavily on subcontractors for PM expertise.



7.2.6 Added Value
Added value is defined as incremental improvements to a project deliverable that improve performance or provide a significant business advantage, for which the client is willing to pay. Project managers seek added-value opportunities that benefit the client.

Chapter 1 Recap

Project management is the planning, organizing, directing, and controlling of resources for a short-term, specific objective.

Modern project success is defined by delivering intended business value and adhering to competing constraints, not just time, cost, and scope.

The Talent Triangle identifies necessary skills: Technical Project Management, Leadership, and Strategic and Business Management.

The Project Manager acts as a general manager and a primary planner, using a systems approach to integrate multifunctional activities.

Projects exist within either project-driven or non–project-driven organizational contexts, which affects how resources and accountability are managed.

Scope changes are inevitable but must be minimized and mutually agreed upon.

The PM-LM interface involves a split in authority, requiring the PM to manage by influence rather than formal control in many cases.

title: 7  Project Management
section: 7.3 Project Management Growth: Concepts and Definitions
text:
7.3 Project Management Growth: Concepts and Definitions

7.3.1 Systems, Programs, and Projects: A Definition
A system is a group of organized elements (human or nonhuman) acting as a whole to achieve a common goal. Systems are classified as closed (isolated from the environment) or open (interacting with the environment, like social systems).
A program is typically longer in duration than a project and often consists of multiple projects. Projects are usually the first level of breakdown of a program.


7.3.2 Maturity and Excellence: A Definition
Maturity in project management is the implementation of a standard methodology and accompanying processes that result in a high likelihood of repeated successes. Maturity requires the proper foundation of tools, techniques, processes, and culture.
Organizations excellent in project management are those that create an environment characterized by a continuous stream of successfully managed projects, where success is measured by what is in the best interest of both the company and the project (i.e., customer).

Excellence goes beyond maturity; maturity is a prerequisite for achieving repetitive excellence.



7.3.3 The Many Faces of Success and Failure
Success can be defined subjectively by the stakeholders. The definition of success can be viewed as a cube defined by Time, Cost, and Quality (or Scope), with the optimal outcome being a point within that cube [56, 57f].

Primary success factors are usually seen through the eyes of the customer (e.g., within time, cost, and quality limits; accepted by customer).

Secondary success factors are internal benefits (e.g., follow-on work, using the customer name as a reference, commercialization of a product, minimum scope changes).

Project Failure can be categorized by the divergence between planned and actual outcomes [60f]. Planning Failure occurs when objectives are unrealistic or constantly changing (moving targets).

Degrees of Project Results:

Complete success: Met success criteria, created value, adhered to all constraints.

Partial success: Met success criteria, client accepted deliverables, created value, but one or more constraints were missed.

Partial failure: Not completed as expected (e.g., canceled early), but knowledge or intellectual property was created for future use.




7.3.4 Life-Cycle Phases
The theoretical life-cycle phases of a system applied to a project include:

Conceptual: Preliminary idea evaluation, risk analysis, and feasibility study.

Planning: Detailed breakdown of work, establishing budgets and schedules.

Testing: Verification of performance and requirements.

Implementation (Execution): Performing the work.

Closure: Final verification, contractual, financial, and administrative closure.

Closure includes verifying all work is accomplished, contractual closure, financial closure (closing charge numbers), and administrative closure (paperwork).


7.3.5 Project Management Methodologies to Frameworks

Methodology: A repetitive, standard process often based on rigid policies and procedures, successful primarily when requirements are well-defined (e.g., Waterfall approach).

Framework: A basic conceptual structure providing a skeleton support structure (set of assumptions, concepts, templates, values, processes) that offers the flexibility project teams need to customize their approach to unique project requirements.

If requirements are unclear or likely to change (e.g., software development), an Agile approach, where work evolves over the project life cycle, may be needed instead of the rigid Waterfall approach.


7.3.6 Growth of Strategic Project Management
PM is increasingly applied to strategic projects (focused on long-term goals and objectives, often with vague business cases) rather than only traditional operational projects (focused on deliverables, clear objectives). This trend has necessitated the use of flexible frameworks over rigid methodologies.


7.3.7 Lean Project Management
Lean Project Management focuses on eliminating waste and maximizing effectiveness and efficiency, often contrasting with the documentation-heavy, sequential nature of the Waterfall approach.







Traditional (Waterfall) Approach
Lean Approach




Massive project documentation
Minimal documentation


Excessive number of customer-contractor team meetings
Minimal number of team meetings


Sponsorship at the executive levels
Sponsorship at lower and middle levels





7.3.8 Systems Thinking
The systems approach is a logical and disciplined process of problem-solving that requires analyzing the total project rather than individual parts. It involves translating requirements into objectives, analyzing alternatives and constraints, synthesizing solutions, and performing trade-offs [97f].

Chapter 2 Recap

Maturity involves using standardized processes; Excellence is achieving a continuous stream of successful projects.

Project results are classified as complete success, partial success, or partial failure.

Projects move through life-cycle phases (Conceptual, Planning, Testing, Implementation, Closure), often marked by “gates” or “on-off ramps” for control.

The rigidity of Methodologies (Waterfall) contrasts with the flexibility of Frameworks (Agile/Hybrid), which allow tailoring to unique projects.

PM is increasingly used for Strategic Projects linked to organizational business models.

Systems Thinking is essential for analyzing the totality of project needs, integrating alternatives, requirements, objectives, and constraints.

Project management requires building a knowledge repository that captures both project and business best practices.

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.4 Organizational Structures

7.4.1 The Traditional (Functional) Organization
In a traditional structure (pure functional organization), management is arranged hierarchically by specialty (e.g., Engineering, Marketing, Finance) [111, 111f].

Advantages: Strong concentration of technical expertise, easy career progression for specialists, and suitability for mass production.

Disadvantages (for projects): Difficult project integration, lack of a single customer contact point, poor response time, and lack of horizontal authority/responsibility.



7.4.2 Matrix Organizational Form
The matrix structure attempts to combine the advantages of pure functional and product organizational structures and is ideally suited for project-driven companies.

The project manager reports directly to the General Manager and has total responsibility and accountability for project success (the horizontal hierarchy).

Functional departments maintain technical excellence (the vertical hierarchy).

Ground Rules for Matrix Development:

Participants are full-time on the project (for loyalty).

Horizontal and vertical channels must exist for commitments.

Quick and effective methods for conflict resolution must exist.

Good communication and free access between managers are required.

All managers must input into the planning process.

Managers must be willing to negotiate.




7.4.3 Project Management Offices (PMOs)
The Project Management Office (PMO) concept is considered one of the most important PM activities for achieving professionalism, maturity, and excellence in project management. PMOs can be designed for specific business purposes, each potentially having unique missions, goals, and strategic business objectives.

Organizations can maintain several types of PMOs, sharing information like metrics and KPIs.



7.4.4 Common Project Management Implementation Fallacies
Fallacies are mistaken beliefs that delay PM maturity.

Fallacy 1: Our Ultimate Goal Is to Implement Project Management. Wrong goal; the goal is the progressive development of systems and processes that consistently produce a continuous stream of successful projects.

Fallacy 2: We Need to Establish a Mandatory Number of Forms, Templates, Guidelines, and Checklists.Wrong criteria; maturity is evaluated by time-based levels and assessment instruments. Focus should be on developing an organization-wide methodology that receives buy-in.

Fallacy 3: We Need to Purchase Project Management Software to Accelerate the Maturity Process. Wrong approach; software selection should be based on benefits like efficiency and consistency, not just packaged features.

Fallacy 4: We Need to Implement Project Management in Small Steps with a Small Breakthrough Project.Wrong method; using a large, successfully managed project as a breakthrough implies the processes work on small projects, but the reverse is not necessarily true.

Fallacy 6: We Need Executive Support. Almost true; visible executive support is required. Executives must “walk the talk” and maintain an open-door policy.

Fallacy 7: We Need a Project Management Course so Our Workers Can Become Project Management Professionals (PMPs). Getting closer; lifelong education is needed, as becoming a PMP is just the starting point.


Chapter 3 Recap

Organizational structures, ranging from traditional functional organizations to specialized project organizations and PMOs, dictate the flow of authority and responsibility.

The Matrix Organization attempts to balance technical expertise (vertical) with project accountability (horizontal).

The PMO is crucial for standardizing PM processes, sharing knowledge, and providing strategic planning for project management.

Successful PM implementation is hindered by common fallacies, such as believing that software or mandatory documentation ensures maturity.

Organizational structure selection is based on factors like project size, length, cost, and upper management’s philosophy.

title: 7  Project Management
section: 7.5 Organizing and Staffing the Project Office and Team
text:
7.5 Organizing and Staffing the Project Office and Team

7.5.1 Selecting the Project Manager: An Executive Decision
Project management success depends heavily on having a good project manager (PM) at the controls. Senior management must visibly appoint the PM and delegate the necessary authority.
The major responsibilities of the PM include ensuring resources are controlled within cost, time, and performance. PMs also must define the specific constraints, priorities, and internal/external interfaces for their project.


7.5.2 Skill Requirements for Project and Program Managers
Effective PM requires proficiency in 10 key skills, similar to the eight performance domains of the PMBOK® Guide, 7th Edition. These skills are:

Team-Building Skills: Nurturing a climate conducive to teamwork, commitment, good interpersonal relations, open communication, and supportive top management.

Leadership Skills: Managing human and nonhuman resources to accomplish tasks and meet project constraints.

Conflict Resolution Skills: Resolving conflicts, often informally, due to the constant nature of project conflicts.

Technical Skills: Understanding the technology of the project and acting as the final technical authority.

Planning Skills: Absolutely essential for complex projects, requiring communication and information processing to define resource requirements and negotiating commitments.

Organizational Skills: Understanding the complex interfaces and relationships between the project office, functional departments, and management.

Administrative Skills: Managing administrative tasks like budgets, schedules, contracts, and performance appraisals.

Communications Skills: Essential for effective interpersonal relations and timely communication.

Negotiating Skills: Securing resources and commitments with little or no formal authority.

Time Management Skills: Required to manage a turbulent environment filled with meetings, reports, and crisis management.



7.5.3 The Organizational Staffing Process
The staffing process must answer three questions:

What resources are required?

Where will the people come from?

What type of project organizational structure will be best?

Continuity of staffing is critical. The decision to accept an average worker who will be available for the entire project versus fighting for the “best” resources for short-term tasks presents a continuous trade-off.


7.5.4 The Project Office (PO)
The Project Office (PO) typically consists of personnel assigned full-time to a project, working outside the functional units.
Major Responsibilities of the PO:

Acting as the focal point of information for internal control and customer reporting.

Controlling time, cost, and performance to adhere to contractual requirements.

Ensuring all work required is documented and distributed.

Ensuring all work performed is authorized and funded.

Integration of work across functional lines—the most common cause of project failure is a lack of proper integration.


Chapter 4 Recap

Project success is contingent upon visible executive appointment and delegated authority to the PM.

PMs require a broad array of skills beyond technical competence, especially focused on leadership, communication, planning, and conflict resolution.

The staffing process involves crucial decisions regarding the needed resources, their source, and organizational structure.

The Project Office (PO) is dedicated to the management of a single large project, focusing on integration and control.

Full-time PO personnel are essential for effective integration, scheduling under uncertainty, communication, and control.

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.6 Management Functions

7.6.1 Management Functions
The traditional classical management functions still apply to PM, though their application differs for temporary management positions:

Planning

Organizing

Staffing

Controlling

Directing



7.6.2 Directing
Directing is the implementing and carrying out (through others) of approved plans. It involves:

Staffing: Selecting qualified personnel.

Training: Teaching duties and responsibilities.

Supervising: Giving guidance and discipline.

Delegating: Assigning work, responsibility, and authority.

Coordinating: Integrating tasks across functional boundaries.

Counseling: Advising, assisting, and helping solve job-related issues.

PMs must be decisive; it is better to decide and be slightly wrong than to wait for complete information and cause a delay. Directives should apply the KISS (Keep It Simple, Stupid) rule.


7.6.3 Power, Authority, and the Project Manager
Authority is the legitimate right to give orders. In PM, authority is generally delegated by senior management and documented in the project charter and job description.
Power is the capacity to influence behavior. The sources of power for a project manager include:

Punishment Power (Coercive Power): The ability to bring about undesirable events.

Reward Power: The ability to provide positive benefits.

Expert Power: The command of technology or specialized knowledge.

Referent Power: The power derived from personality or charisma.

Legitimate Power: Formal authority granted by position.



7.6.4 Leadership in a Project Environment
Leadership is a style of behavior designed to integrate organizational requirements and personal interests toward an objective. Leadership involves balancing three complex elements: the person leading, the people being led, and the situation (the project environment).

PMs often fail when they cannot balance the technical and managerial functions.

Successful leadership requires PMs to understand the changing values and potential conflicts between the project manager, project team, parent organization, and stakeholders.



7.6.5 General Management Pitfalls
Common management pitfalls in the project environment include:

Lack of self-control (knowing oneself): Overestimating one’s capabilities and attempting tasks without sufficient time for training or support.

Activity traps: When the means become the end (e.g., endless team meetings, unnecessary documentation, developing charts only for upper management).

Management bottlenecks.


Chapter 5 Recap

Classical management functions (Planning, Organizing, Staffing, Controlling, Directing) are applied differently in temporary project environments.

The Directing function includes staffing, training, supervising, delegating, coordinating, and counseling.

Project managers rely on various forms of power (Expert, Reward, Referent) to compensate for often limited legitimate authority.

Effective Leadership requires integrating the interests of the organization and the individual within the project environment.

Common pitfalls include falling into activity traps (means becoming the end) and operating without self-control.

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.7 Communications Management

7.7.1 Modeling the Communications Environment
Effective communication ensures the right information reaches the right person at the right time in a cost-effective manner. The communications environment is modeled as a network of channels.

The number of two-way channels (\(N\)) for \(n\) participants is calculated as \(N \= n(n-1)/2\). (Example: 5 people results in 10 channels).

Communication barriers occur due to perception screens (information filtered by education/experience) and personality screens (likes/dislikes) [192, 193f].



7.7.2 Meetings
Meetings are essential for exchanging information, but meeting mania is costly.
General Guides for Effective Meetings:

Start on time.

Develop agenda “objectives.”

Conduct one piece of business at a time.

Allow each member to contribute.

Types of Meetings:

Project Review Meetings: Held periodically (weekly or monthly).

Special Project Meetings: Held on an as-needed basis (e.g., dealing with critical problems).

Customer Project Review Meetings: Often critical and inflexibly scheduled. PMs must prepare materials well in advance.



7.7.3 Using Action Items
To combat meeting mania and excessive costs, detailed action items should be clearly assigned. A critical strategy for customer meetings is to send handouts at least a week before the meeting, allowing the client time to digest the material and prepare to make timely decisions, thus reducing follow-up action items.


7.7.4 Brainstorming
Brainstorming is a technique used to stimulate idea generation and increase group creativity.
Four Basic Rules of Brainstorming:

Focus on quantity: Maximizing possible ideas, assuming greater quantity increases the chance of finding the optimal solution.

Withhold criticism: Criticism creates conflict and wastes time needed for idea generation.

Unusual ideas are welcome: Encourage creativity and risk-taking.

Combine and improve ideas: Encourage the combination of existing ideas to generate new ones.

Process of Conducting a Brainstorming Session:

Participants who did not speak are encouraged to write down ideas.

The idea collector numbers the ideas to encourage a generation goal (e.g., “let’s get it to 20!”).

The idea collector repeats the idea verbatim to confirm meaning.

When many participants have ideas, the one with the most associated idea should have priority (to encourage elaboration).

Managers/superiors may be discouraged from attending to minimize inhibition.


Chapter 6 Recap

Communication effectiveness is measured by getting the right information to the right person, often limited by personality and perception barriers.

The number of two-way communication channels increases exponentially with the number of participants (\(N = n(n-1)/2\)).

Project managers must actively manage meetings to avoid costly “meeting mania”.

Sending client materials ahead of time reduces costly action items and travel expenses.

Brainstorming relies on four rules, focusing on idea quantity and withholding immediate criticism.

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.8 Conflicts

7.8.1 Types of Conflicts
It is impossible to eliminate all conflicts, and project managers must understand how to manage them.
Most Common Conflict Types:

Staffing resources

Equipment and facilities

Capital expenditures

Costs

Technical opinions and trade-offs

Priorities (most frequently occurring)

Administrative procedures

Scheduling (most frequently occurring)

Responsibilities

Personality clashes (potentially most damaging).

Conflicts vary in intensity over the project life cycle, often peaking during the buildup/main phase [209f].


7.8.2 Conflict Management Procedures (Minimization)
Project managers should follow conflict minimization procedures, including:

Pausing and thinking before reacting.

Building trust and cooperation.

Trying to understand the conflict motives.

Keeping the meeting under control.

Listening to all involved parties.

Maintaining a give-and-take attitude.

Being willing to admit fault.

Conflict Resolution Modes (commonly identified in management texts):

Compromising: Giving up something to achieve a partial solution.

Smoothing (Accommodation): Emphasizing common areas of agreement.

Forcing (Competition): Using power to impose a solution.

Withdrawal (Avoidance): Retreating from conflict.

Confrontation (Problem Solving): Facing the conflict directly to reach a win-win solution.


Chapter 7 Recap

Conflicts are a continuous part of the project environment, often involving priorities, scheduling, staffing, and personality clashes.

The intensity of conflicts varies throughout the project life cycle [209f].

The most frequent conflicts often occur with functional managers and technical personnel, usually over schedules, priorities, and manpower [210f].

Effective conflict management includes understanding motives, building trust, and utilizing appropriate resolution modes like confrontation/problem-solving.

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.9 Special Topics

7.9.1 Performance Measurement
Functional employees working on projects can be evaluated in several ways:

A functional manager evaluates the employee with PM input.

PM evaluates the employee with LM input.

The PM evaluates the employee, and the LM signs off.

LM evaluates the employee without PM input (common in weak matrix).

The PM makes the entire evaluation (if the employee is 100% full-time on the project).

All PMs and LMs jointly evaluate all employees (limited to small companies).

Metrics for Managerial Performance include overall project management effectiveness, direction, and team performance.


7.9.2 Financial Compensation and Rewards
Project compensation systems are based on four foundations: job classification, base pay, performance appraisals, and merit increases.

Base Pay Classifications and Incentives: Project managers whose performance is measured by business results (profit, ROI, new business) often receive higher compensation. Managerial performance (effectiveness, leadership) is harder to assess.

Incentive Plans: Used to maximize efficiency and effectiveness. Incentives can be monetary or nonmonetary and are often tied to project completion, cost reduction, or added value.



7.9.3 Mega Projects
Mega projects are large, long-duration projects characterized by immense scale, design and construction complexity, high public profile, multiple stakeholders, and frequent cost underestimation.

Success requires extensive project management training, clearly defined rules/procedures, strong communications, and quality front-end planning.



7.9.4 Morality, Ethics, and the Corporate Culture
The PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct is divided into four sections:

Responsibility: Taking ownership for decisions and their consequences.

Respect: Regarding ourselves and the resources provided to us.

Fairness: Making impartial, objective decisions free from prejudice.

Honesty: Understanding the truth and communicating it truthfully.



7.9.5 Agile Project Management
Agile is iterative, intending to finish deliverables quickly using successive cycles of continuous improvement.







Traditional PM
Agile Practices




Planning prior to execution; Follow the plan exactly
Iterative planning; Respond to changes


Single release at the end of the project (often late delivery)
Multiple releases of a usable product; Shorter delivery time


Too much “gold-plating” (unused features)
Minimal number of features (What the client needs)





7.9.6 Artificial Intelligence (AI)
Advances in AI (including expert and knowledge-based systems) offer PMs the promise of enhanced decision-making. For AI to be beneficial, organizations must consolidate their “pockets” of project management knowledge into a corporate-wide knowledge management system (intellectual property), often managed by the PMO.

Chapter 8 Recap

Employee performance measurement in a project environment is complicated by dual reporting relationships, requiring coordination between PMs and LMs.

Financial rewards can be linked to business results (e.g., ROI, profit) or managerial performance (effectiveness).

Mega Projects demand intense management rigor due to their complexity, risk, and duration.

Ethical conduct, defined by responsibility, respect, fairness, and honesty, is critical for project success.

Agile methodologies prioritize iteration, frequent releases, and customer collaboration, contrasting with traditional sequential approaches.

The integration of AI requires robust knowledge management systems to provide the necessary intellectual property for effective tools.

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.10 The Variables for Success

7.10.1 Project Management Effectiveness Variables
Successful PM cannot be achieved simply by authority or executive meddling; it requires a strong corporate commitment and environment. Key PM effectiveness variables include:

Credibility: Derived from being a sound decision-maker, emphasizing facts, and making success visible.

Priority: Selling the importance of the project to the total organization’s objectives.

Accessibility: The ability to communicate directly with top management.

Visibility: Being aware of necessary visibility and conducting timely informational meetings.



7.10.2 Understanding Best Practices
Best practices are actions or activities that lead to a sustained competitive advantage in project management. They are often referred to as proven practices.
Levels of Best Practices (from general to specific application) [331f]:

Professional (PMI) Standards (Highest quantity, lowest complexity).

Industry Standards (Specific to an industry, e.g., automotive).

Company-Specific (Internal organizational standards).

Project-Specific (Unique to a single project).

Individual (Lowest quantity, highest complexity).


7.10.2.1 Creating a Best Practices Library
The organizational solution for retaining PM knowledge is creating a best practices library.
Steps for Creating a Best Practices Library [333f]:

Discovery and Understanding: Identifying potential best practices (BP) from within the organization.

Evaluation: Determining if the action is a BP.

Classification: Categorizing the BP (e.g., by Project Management, Customer Interfacing, Business Applications) [334f].

Storage: Placing the BP into a retrieval system (e.g., company intranet).

Knowledge Transfer: Disseminating the information (e.g., intranet, seminars, case studies).


Chapter 9 Recap

Project success depends on establishing an atmosphere conducive to good working relationships between the project, the parent organization, and the client.

PM effectiveness variables include Credibility, Priority, Accessibility, and Visibility.

Project closure must include planning for personnel disposition, material disposal, knowledge transfer, and financial closeout.

Best Practices are actions leading to a competitive advantage, classified across five levels from professional standards to individual practices.

A Best Practices Library is the critical tool for capturing and transferring project and business knowledge across the organization.

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.11 Working with Executives

7.11.1 The Project Sponsor
The project sponsor is the ultimate owner of the project and has the ultimate objective of providing behind-the-scenes assistance to project personnel.
Sponsor Responsibilities:

Providing guidance and mentorship.

Resolving conflicts and making timely project decisions.

Serving as the focal point for project status for other executives.

Establishing a structured policy for scope changes.

Delineating expectations for the PM and team.

Project managers should manage the relationship with the sponsor, but employees should be careful about how often they “go to the well” (bypass management levels).


7.11.2 Stakeholder Relations Management
Stakeholders are individuals or groups who have something to lose or gain from the project outcome. Stakeholders are identified by individuals/titles, contribution (financial, resource, technology), or authority.

7.11.2.1 Stakeholder Analysis and Mapping
The project manager must prioritize stakeholders to manage interactions effectively. Stakeholder mapping typically displays stakeholders on a grid comparing their power and their level of interest.








Quadrant
Power/Interest
Strategy




Manage Closely
High Power, High Interest
Put forth the greatest effort to satisfy them; they can make or break the project.


Keep Satisfied
High Power, Low Interest
Put forth effort, but avoid excessive detail that leads to boredom.


Keep Informed
Low Power, High Interest
Adequate communication to avoid sudden high interest due to surprise.


Monitor
Low Power, Low Interest
Minimum effort required; monitor them occasionally.






7.11.3 Project Portfolio Management (PPM)
PPM is a practice to alleviate organizational problems such as misalignment of projects to business strategies and working on non–value-added projects. A portfolio is a grouping of projects and programs, usually those that are considered strategic.
Governance Committee Assessment (PPM):

Assess weak investments (cancellation or replacement).

Determine if consolidation is needed.

Determine if projects need acceleration or deceleration.

Assess alignment to strategic objectives and rebalance the portfolio.


Chapter 10 Recap

The Project Sponsor guides the project, resolves high-level conflicts, and ensures alignment with organizational goals.

Project managers must focus on managing the sponsor’s expectations and supporting them, especially in times of crisis.

Stakeholder Management requires identifying individuals, analyzing their needs, and strategically engaging them based on their power and interest.

Portfolio Management ensures that groups of projects and programs are aligned with strategic objectives and maximize value for the firm.

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.12 Planning

7.12.1 Business Case
Projects usually begin with a business case, a document providing the reasoning why a project should be initiated. It justifies the project by showing that expected business value and benefits exceed the cost of performance.
Key Components of a Business Case:

Assumptions made (all assumptions justifying the project).

High-level or strategic objectives.

Recommended evaluation techniques (e.g., benefit-to-cost ratio, NPV, ROI).

Project metrics (financial and nonfinancial).

Exit strategies (cancellation criteria).

Project risks, complexity, resources needed, timing, and legal requirements.



7.12.2 Validating the Assumptions
Assumptions (items believed to be true but not grounded in fact) carry risk and often lead to unmet expectations if they change.

Explicit assumptions are quantified and unambiguous.

Implicit assumptions are hidden within explicit statements (e.g., assuming five assigned people will have the necessary skills).

Critical assumptions can cause significant damage if even small changes occur and must be tracked closely.



7.12.3 Objectives and the SMART Rule
Project objectives should be clearly written and understood so the team knows when the project is over. Objectives must follow the SMART rule:

Specific

Measurable

Attainable

Realistic or Relevant

Tangible or Time bound.

If requirements are unrealistic, scaling back the scope (reducing product functionality) is often the first step, though this must be done carefully to maintain product value.


7.12.4 The Statement of Work (SOW)
The Statement of Work (SOW) is a narrative description of the work required for the project and the end results to be provided under the contract.

For external projects, two SOWs exist: the SOW used in the proposal and the Contract Statement of Work (CSOW).

Common causes of SOW misinterpretation include mixing tasks/specifications, using imprecise language (e.g., “optimum,” “approximately”), and failing to get third-party review.



7.12.5 Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)
